children.name: Recurvirostra
children.description: Elegant pied waders with distinctively upturned bills swept through shallow water to catch invertebrates. The Pied Avocet breeds in shallow saline lagoons in southern Sweden — Öland and Gotland — and is expanding northward.

children.name: Recurvirostra avosetta
children.description: An elegant black-and-white wader that sweeps its slender upturned bill sideways through shallow water to filter out invertebrates. It breeds in saline lagoons on Öland, Gotland, and the southern coast and is expanding northward.

children.name: Recurvirostra americana
children.description: A striking wader of western North American wetlands with a rusty-orange head and neck in breeding plumage and a strongly upturned bill swept through shallow alkaline water.

children.name: Recurvirostra novaehollandiae
children.description: An Australian avocet with a deep chestnut head and neck, frequenting shallow saline lakes and estuaries across the inland and coast.

children.name: Recurvirostra andina
children.description: A high-altitude avocet of saline lakes in the Andean altiplano of Peru, Bolivia, Chile, and Argentina, adapted to cold, mineral-rich waters.

children.name: Himantopus himantopus
children.description: The black-winged stilt is a widely distributed, very long-legged wader in the avocet and stilt family Recurvirostridae. Its scientific name, Himantopus himantopus, is sometimes used to generalize a single, almost cosmopolitan species. Alternatively, it is restricted to the form that is widespread in Europe, Asia and Africa, which equals the nominate group of H.

children.name: Himantopus mexicanus
children.description: The black-necked stilt, also called the pied stilt, is a locally abundant shorebird of North and South American wetlands and coastlines. It is found from the coastal areas of California through much of the interior western United States and along the Gulf of Mexico as far east as Florida, then south through Central America and the Caribbean to Brazil, Peru and the Galápagos Islands, with an isolated population, the Hawaiian stilt, in Hawaii. The northernmost populations, particularly those from inland, are migratory, wintering from the extreme south of the United States to southern Mexico, rarely as far south as Costa Rica; on the Baja California peninsula it is only found regularly in winter.

children.name: Himantopus leucocephalus
children.description: A long-legged stilt of Australasia and Southeast Asia, distinguished by its white head and black hindneck patch.

children.name: Himantopus novaezelandiae
children.description: An all-black stilt endemic to New Zealand's South Island braided rivers; one of the world's rarest wading birds, Critically Endangered with intensive captive-breeding recovery efforts.

children.name: Cladorhynchus
children.description: A monotypic genus of nomadic Australian wader that breeds explosively on ephemeral inland salt lakes.

children.name: Cladorhynchus leucocephalus
children.description: A nomadic Australian wader with a chestnut breast band, breeding in vast colonies on temporary salt lakes triggered by inland flooding and brine-shrimp blooms.

children.name: Ibidorhyncha
children.description: A distinctive monotypic Asian wader of high mountain rivers, sometimes placed in its own family.

children.name: Ibidorhyncha struthersii
children.description: A grey wader with a long downcurved crimson bill and black face, inhabiting shingle riverbeds of the high Himalayas and Central Asian mountains.
